Occasionally, multinucleated giant cells and cytoplasmic melanin pigments are also present.
cleaned: Tumor containing highly anaplastic, discohesive cells without a specific growth pattern. Cells possess abundant acidophilic, finely granular, dense cytoplasm, and prominent macronucleoli. Occasionally, multinucleated giant cells and cytoplasmic melanin pigments are present. Anorectal melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) of an enlarged left inguinal lymph node, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at ×100 magnification. The smear shows highly cellular pleomorphic cells arranged in small clusters, groups, and individually scattered, admixed with lymphocytes. The inset further highlights individual cells, which are large and pleomorphic, possessing a centrally placed, hyperchromatic nucleus with abundant cytoplasm. Some cells exhibit an eccentrically placed nucleus and prominent nucleoli, and a few binucleated cells are also visible. These features are suggestive of secondary deposits in the lymph node, consistent with the patient's provisional diagnosis of amelanotic malignant melanoma.
cleaned: Highly cellular pleomorphic cells arranged in small clusters, groups, and individually scattered, admixed with lymphocytes. Large and pleomorphic cells with centrally placed, hyperchromatic nuclei and abundant cytoplasm. Some cells exhibit eccentrically placed nuclei and prominent nucleoli; a few binucleated cells are visible. Malignant melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of the excised pigmented lesion from the cheek of a middle-aged woman, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) and viewed on low-power (×10). This section reveals atypical intraepidermal melanocytic proliferation overlying dermal elastosis, consistent with a diagnosis of melanoma in situ on sun-damaged skin. The gold standard for LM/LMM diagnosis is skin biopsy, allowing visualization of dermatopathologic features like melanocyte atypia and non-uniform distribution on a background of extensive photodamage.
cleaned: Atypical intraepidermal melanocytic proliferation overlying dermal elastosis. Melanoma in situ.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of the excised pigmented lesion from the cheek of a middle-aged woman, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) and viewed on higher-power (×20). This magnification provides a clearer view of the atypical intraepidermal melanocytic proliferation, which was found to extend to all surgical margins, leading to a diagnosis of melanoma in situ on sun-damaged skin. Key dermatopathologic features consistent with this diagnosis include melanocyte atypia and their non-uniform distribution within the epidermis, often seen with extensive photodamage.
cleaned:
Atypical melanocytic proliferation extending into the epidermis. Melanocytes exhibit atypia and non-uniform distribution. Sun-damaged skin.

Melanoma in situ.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of the excised pigmented lesion from the cheek of a middle-aged woman, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) and viewed on high-power (×40). The presence of a pagetoid appearance of melanocytes, melanocyte atypia, and non-uniform pigmentation and distribution of melanocytes on a background of extensive photodamage are characteristic dermatopathologic features for melanoma in situ.
cleaned: Atypical intraepidermal melanocytic proliferation with dermal elastosis. Pagetoid appearance of melanocytes, melanocyte atypia, and non-uniform pigmentation. Extensive photodamage. Melanoma in situ.

label: melanoma

label: melanoma
original: Histopathological examination from a 76-year-old man with nodular-type malignant melanoma (MM) of the lower anterior alveolar gingiva. cleaned: Nodular-type malignant melanoma. Intact surgical margin. Reactive bone trabeculae. Lesion does not extend to the bottom border.

label: nevus

label: melanoma
original: Histological study showing malignant melanoma, characterized by a mutation of exon 15 in the BRAF gene, with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain at 40x magnification. The patient was diagnosed with primary cerebral melanoma.
cleaned: Malignant melanoma. Atypical melanocytes with pleomorphic nuclei are present, infiltrating the surrounding tissue. Lesion exhibits irregular borders and varied pigmentation.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology showing a blue nevus. The tissue section is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) and viewed at 40x magnification.
cleaned: Blue nevus. Spindle and nevus cells with abundant blue-toned cytoplasm are arranged in fascicles within the dermis.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of vulvar melanoma in situ. cleaned: Broad, asymmetric, junctional melanocytic proliferation with variable epidermal acanthosis and dermal clusters of heavily pigmented melanophages. Enlarged, poorly nested and confluent, severely atypical melanocytes. Melanoma in situ.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of vulvar melanoma in situ. Photomicrograph of the excised specimen showing confluent, severely atypical melanocytes with pagetoid spread. cleaned: Confluent, severely atypical melanocytes with pagetoid spread. Enlarged, poorly nested and confluent, severely atypical melanocytes. Melanoma in situ.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of vulvar melanoma in situ. Photomicrograph of the excised specimen showing areas with lichenoid lymphoid infiltrates and dermal melanophages. cleaned: Lichenoid lymphoid infiltrates and dermal melanophages are visible. Areas of heavily pigmented cells are present. Diagnosis: melanoma in situ.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of vulvar melanoma in situ. label: nevus

label: melanoma

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing the radial growth phase (RGP) in primary cutaneous malignant melanoma. Stained with hematoxylin/eosin at 4× magnification.
cleaned: Malignant melanoma exhibiting radial growth phase. Proliferation of transformed melanocytes above the epidermal basement membrane with a lentiginous or pagetoid pattern.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing the vertical growth phase (VGP) in primary cutaneous malignant melanoma. Stained with hematoxylin/eosin at 4× magnification.
cleaned: Malignant melanoma exhibiting vertical growth phase. Deep dermal and subcutaneous extension of tumor cells. Variable cell morphology with atypical nuclei. Lesion displays irregular patterns.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology showing pleomorphic and vesicular nuclei, evident nucleoli, and occasional mitosis (<2/mm 2), features compatible with atypical cellular blue nevus. (Hematoxylin & eosin x400 magnification)

label: nevus

label: melanoma

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of a lesion from the scalp of a 52-year-old male, diagnosed by three dermatopathologists as malignant melanoma in situ. This lesion was initially submitted as lentigo maligna, which is a subtype where false-negative results for the diagnostic test were most common. This specific case represents a false-negative result, as the diagnostic test yielded a score of -2.3 (indicating a negative result), despite the expert consensus diagnosis of malignant melanoma in situ. Hematoxylin-eosin stain; original magnification x20.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of a lesion from the scalp of a 52-year-old male, diagnosed by three dermatopathologists as malignant melanoma in situ. Spindle and epithelioid cells with abundant cytoplasm arranged in nests and fascicles. The lesion demonstrates a symmetrical architecture with maturation with descent. Melanocytes are present with large nuclei and prominent nucleoli. No mitoses are identified.

original: Microscopic examination of a lentigo maligna melanoma biopsy shows atypical melanocytes along the dermal-epidermal junction. There is also extension of the tumor cells down adnexal structures (hematoxylin and eosin; magnification x20).

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing a UV-induced melanoma obtained from an HGF/SF (Hepatocyte growth factor/scatter factor)-overexpression mouse model. The mice were irradiated at post-natal day four with 4 kJ/m2 of ultraviolet light (UV) (61% UVB, 28.3% UVA, 2.09% UVC). This image displays the morphology of a heavily pigmented tumor, which is strikingly similar to melanomas observed in rodents. Characteristically, these tumors display a proliferation of dendritic or spindled heavily pigmented melanocytes, often with epithelioid cells in the dermis and infiltrative borders. The brown color visible in the section represents melanin pigment. These lesions develop into heavily pigmented tumors with dendritic, spindle, and some epithelioid cells invading the dermis and adjacent structures. Despite the potentially benign-looking or borderline morphology, these tumors are known to metastasize. Stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E). Microscopic magnification: ×10.
cleaned:
Heavily pigmented tumor displaying a proliferation of dendritic and spindled melanocytes, with some epithelioid cells. Melanin pigment is visible as brown coloration. Lesions invade the dermis with infiltrative borders. 

Diagnosis: melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing a UV-induced melanoma obtained from an HGF/SF (Hepatocyte growth factor/scatter factor)-overexpression mouse model. The mice were irradiated at post-natal day four with 4 kJ/m2 of ultraviolet light (UV) (61% UVB, 28.3% UVA, 2.09% UVC). This image displays the morphology of a heavily pigmented tumor, which is strikingly similar to melanomas observed in rodents. Characteristically, these tumors display a proliferation of dendritic or spindled heavily pigmented melanocytes, often with epithelioid cells in the dermis and infiltrative borders. The brown color visible in the section represents melanin pigment. These tumors are observed to have infiltrative borders and contain dendritic, spindle, and some epithelioid cells invading the dermis and adjacent structures. Despite the potentially benign-looking or borderline morphology, these tumors are known to metastasize and cause mortality in the animals. Stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E). Microscopic magnification: ×40.
cleaned:
Heavily pigmented tumor displaying a proliferation of dendritic and spindled melanocytes, with some epithelioid cells. Brown pigment is visible throughout the tumor. Infiltrative borders are present with cells invading the dermis. 

Melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of cutaneous melanoma cells, stained with H&E, showing a small number of melanoma cells with visible nucleoli. label: melanoma

The image shows a poorly pigmented polypoid vital tumor mass surrounded by a well-defined and heavily pigmented necrotic “ring.” Radiation-induced necrosis is characterized by a sharp demarcation from the residual viable tumor tissue and by a high degree of pigmentation, often higher than that of the surrounding viable tumor, due to abundant infiltration of melanophages within necrotic areas. Radiation-induced necrosis is characterized by a sharp and well-defined demarcation from the residual viable tumor tissue and a high degree of pigmentation, often more pronounced than the surrounding viable tumor, due to copious melanophage infiltration. This patient underwent secondary enucleation 29 months after proton beam radiotherapy due to tumor regrowth.
cleaned: Abrupt transition between viable tumor at the bottom and necrosis at the top. Necrosis shows a sharp demarcation and high pigmentation due to melanophage infiltration. Choroidal melanoma.

In the majority of cases, radiation-induced necrosis is characterized by this sharp demarcation from residual viable tumor tissue and by a high degree of pigmentation (higher than that of surrounding viable tumor), due to an abundant infiltration of melanophages within necrotic areas. These lesions tend to be symmetric with an infiltrative base, and while mitoses are usually rare and not atypical, moderate cytological atypia can be present in a significant percentage of HRAS-mutated cases.

Fascicles of large spindle melanocytes with amphophilic cytoplasm, moderately pleomorphic vesicular nuclei, and distinct nucleoli. Thick collagen bundles are intermingled, creating a desmoplastic appearance. 

Spitz nevus.

label: nevus
original: Photomicrograph of a Spitz nevus harboring ALK fusion, showing a large, exophytic and dermal melanocytic proliferation with an infiltrative base. Histologically, most of these lesions share a distinctive pattern substantiated by a compound wedge shape silhouette with a bulbous and/or infiltrative base. The proliferation is composed of nonpigmented, large, epithelioid and spindle melanocytes with amphophilic cytoplasm, vesicular nuclei, and prominent nucleoli, arranged in plexiform intersecting fascicles. label: nevus
original: Photomicrograph of a Spitz nevus harboring ALK fusion, showing a dermal melanocytic proliferation composed of nonpigmented, large, epithelioid and spindle melanocytes with amphophilic cytoplasm, vesicular nuclei, and prominent nucleoli, arranged in plexiform intersecting fascicles. Diagnosis: Spitz nevus.

label: nevus

label: nevus
original: Photomicrograph showing a prototypical Spitz nevus, a benign melanocytic proliferation. The lesion often presents with a dome-shaped, wedge-shaped, or plaque-like silhouette, characterized by sharp lateral borders and maturation towards the deep part. Spitz nevus.

label: nevus
original: Photomicrograph illustrating a prototypical Spitz nevus, a benign melanocytic proliferation. This view (original magnification 100×) shows the characteristic architectural and cytological features, including large clefting nests of spindle and epithelioid melanocytes with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm. The lesion exhibits epidermal hyperplasia, and the overlying epidermis may be separated from the junctional nests and single melanocytes by clefts. Nests often display varying cellularity, size, and shape, and are typically arranged in parallel to the rete ridges. Scattered Kamino bodies, which are dull eosinophilic globules composed of amorphous filaments, are also present. The melanocytes feature moderately pleomorphic vesicular nuclei and prominent nucleoli. There is a predominance of enlarged epithelioid and/or spindle melanocytes with abundant eosinophilic or amphophilic cytoplasm, often with a “ground-glass” appearance, and round to oval to spindle nuclei with finely dispersed chromatin and distinct nucleoli. The image is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E).

Enlarged epithelioid and spindle melanocytes with eosinophilic or amphophilic cytoplasm, some with a “ground-glass” appearance, and round to oval to spindle nuclei with finely dispersed chromatin and distinct nucleoli.

The overlying epidermis is unremarkable, and no evidence of malignancy is seen. These features are suggestive of a benign hamartomatous lesion, consistent with nevus lipomatosus superficialis. Original magnification 200X.
cleaned: Papillary and reticular dermal lesion composed of mature adipose tissue, with interspersed bands of collagen bundles and scattered sebaceous and sweat glands. Overlying epidermis is unremarkable. Diagnosis: nevus lipomatosus superficialis.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of a biopsy section stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E), showing a papillary and reticular dermal lesion composed of mature adipose tissue. There is no connection with the underlying subcutaneous tissue. Interspersed bands of collagen bundles are present, along with scattered sebaceous and sweat glands exhibiting normal morphology. The overlying epidermis is unremarkable, and no evidence of malignancy is seen. These features are suggestive of a benign hamartomatous lesion, consistent with nevus lipomatosus superficialis. Original magnification 400X.
cleaned: Papillary and reticular dermal lesion composed of mature adipose tissue, with interspersed bands of collagen. Scattered sebaceous and sweat glands with normal morphology are present. Overlying epidermis is unremarkable. Nevus lipomatosus superficialis.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of a skin lesion supporting a diagnosis of Nevus Sebaceous (NS). This image displays epidermal hyperplasia and hyperkeratosis (indicated by red arrow 'A'), consistent with the hyperplastic squamous epithelium observed, which also showed acanthosis and no nuclear abnormalities. Mild papillomatosis and infiltration of lymphocytes in the upper dermis are also visible (indicated by red arrow 'B'). The subdermal area features fibrocollagenous connective tissue with lymphocytic infiltration and vasodilation. Stained with Hematoxylin–eosin at 40× magnification.
cleaned:
Epidermal hyperplasia and hyperkeratosis. Hyperplastic squamous epithelium with acanthosis and no nuclear abnormalities. Mild papillomatosis and lymphocytic infiltration in the upper dermis. Fibrocollagenous connective tissue with lymphocytic infiltration and vasodilation in the subdermal area.
Diagnosis: Nevus Sebaceous.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of a skin lesion supporting a diagnosis of Nevus Sebaceous (NS). This image demonstrates hyperplasia of the sebaceous glands (indicated by red arrow 'A'). The overall histopathological findings for the lesion include hyperplasia of the sebaceous glands, hair follicles, and sudoriferous glands, with no signs of malignancy observed. Stained with Hematoxylin–eosin at 100× magnification.
cleaned: Hyperplasia of sebaceous glands, hair follicles, and sudoriferous glands. Nevus Sebaceous.

label: nevus
original: Microscopy of Case 3, a classical Nevus Lipomatosus Cutaneus Superficialis (NLCS), showing irregular acanthosis, increased capillaries within the papillary dermis, and lobules of adipocytes in the reticular dermis. The image is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 40x magnification.
cleaned:
Irregular acanthosis, increased capillaries within the papillary dermis, and lobules of adipocytes in the reticular dermis.
Diagnosis: Nevus Lipomatosus Cutaneus Superficialis.

label: nevus
original: Microscopy of Case 4, a Nevus Lipomatosus Cutaneus Superficialis (NLCS), displaying a largely unremarkable epidermis, increased dermal vasculature, haphazardly arranged collagen bundles, and adipocytic tissue in the mid and lower dermis. The image is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 40x magnification.
cleaned:
Unremarkable epidermis, increased dermal vasculature, haphazardly arranged collagen bundles, and adipocytic tissue in the mid and lower dermis.
Nevus Lipomatosus Cutaneus Superficialis.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of a 2.5 cm (23 mm) subcutaneous heel mass nodule from a 31-year-old man, showing a dermal nodular mass with fascicles of spindle cells and cellular nests separated by fibrous bands. There is no epidermal component found, with a grenz zone observed. This image was part of the histopathologic examination that led to an initial diagnosis of melanoma, later revised to cutaneous clear-cell sarcoma. Staining is Hematoxylin, Eosin, Saffron (HES), magnified ×40.
cleaned:
Dermal nodular mass with fascicles of spindle cells and cellular nests separated by fibrous bands. No epidermal component is present, with a grenz zone observed. 
Diagnosis: cutaneous clear-cell sarcoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing architectural features of a sinonasal mucosal melanoma, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). The tumor is observed growing as a polypoid, edematous, and highly vascularized mass, with melanoma involving the entire polyp as multiple confluent nodules, reaching at least 1 mm Breslow thickness. The transitional respiratory epithelium is still recognizable all around. Mucosal melanomas are often characterized by significant cytological variability, including spindle, rhabdoid, epithelioid, small, or giant pleomorphic (often multinucleated) cells, which can pose diagnostic challenges in differentiating them from lymphomas, carcinomas, or sarcomas. Necrotic areas are frequently observed, and mitotic activity is typically high. While many mucosal melanomas are associated with an in situ lateral spread on the mucosal surface, their heterogeneous presentation often necessitates immunohistochemical staining (e.g., S100, MART-1, HMB45, or SOX10) to confirm their melanocytic origin.
cleaned:
Polypoid, edematous, and highly vascularized mass with multiple confluent nodules involving the entire polyp. Recognizable transitional respiratory epithelium surrounds the tumor. Cells exhibit significant cytological variability including spindle, rhabdoid, epithelioid, small, and giant pleomorphic (often multinucleated) morphologies. Necrotic areas and high mitotic activity are present. 

Diagnosis: Sinonasal mucosal melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing architectural features of a nodular mucosal melanoma, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). This melanoma arises at the interface between intestinal and squamous perianal epithelium and has a Breslow thickness of 14 mm. Mucosal melanomas are often characterized by significant cytological variability, including spindle, rhabdoid, epithelioid, small, or giant pleomorphic (often multinucleated) cells, which can pose diagnostic challenges in differentiating them from lymphomas, carcinomas, or sarcomas. Necrotic areas are frequently observed, and mitotic activity is typically high. While many mucosal melanomas are associated with an in situ lateral spread on the mucosal surface, their heterogeneous presentation often necessitates immunohistochemical staining (e.g., S100, MART-1, HMB45, or SOX10) to confirm their melanocytic origin.
cleaned: Nodular lesion with significant cytological variability including spindle, rhabdoid, epithelioid, small, and giant pleomorphic cells. Areas of necrosis and high mitotic activity are present. The lesion is located at the interface between intestinal and squamous epithelium. Diagnosis: melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing architectural features of a vaginal nodular mucosal melanoma, stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). The tumor is superficially ulcerated and has a Breslow thickness of 9 mm. Notably, this particular melanoma does not exhibit any visible in situ component in the squamous vaginal epithelium, contrasting with the common finding of an in situ lateral spread often associated with most mucosal melanomas. These tumors are characterized by significant cytological variability, including spindle, rhabdoid, epithelioid, small, or giant pleomorphic (often multinucleated) cells, which can pose diagnostic challenges in differentiating them from lymphomas, carcinomas, or sarcomas. Necrotic areas are frequently observed, and mitotic activity is typically high. Due to their heterogeneous appearance, immunohistochemical staining (e.g., S100, MART-1, HMB45, or SOX10) is often required to confirm their melanocytic origin.
cleaned: Superficially ulcerated nodular lesion with significant cytological variability, including spindle, rhabdoid, epithelioid, small, and giant pleomorphic cells, some multinucleated. Necrotic areas and high mitotic activity are present. Mucosal melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing unusual cytological features of mucosal melanoma in hematoxylin and eosin. This panel displays pleomorphic areas in an epithelioid vulvar melanoma. Epithelioid features were prevalent in 25 out of 33 cases in the study series. The melanocytes are irregularly shaped, and their nuclei appear bizarre. The arrow specifically indicates an abnormal mitotic figure.
cleaned: Irregularly shaped melanocytes with bizarre nuclei. Pleomorphic areas are present. Abnormal mitotic figure visible. Mucosal melanoma. Epithelioid vulvar melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing unusual cytological features of mucosal melanoma in hematoxylin and eosin. This panel illustrates a hyperpigmented genital melanoma, composed of middle-sized, mitotically active spindle cells. In the study series, two cases were composed of spindle hyperpigmented cells.
cleaned: Hyperpigmented lesion composed of middle-sized, mitotically active spindle cells. Mucosal melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing unusual cytological features of mucosal melanoma in hematoxylin and eosin. This panel depicts a rectal melanoma with a rhabdoid appearance, a pattern seen in one case in the study series. The neoplastic cells show a large, eosinophilic cytoplasm and a peripherally located round nucleus.
cleaned: Neoplastic cells with large, eosinophilic cytoplasm and peripherally located round nuclei. Mucosal melanoma with rhabdoid appearance.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of a Spitz nevus (Hematoxylin and Eosin, ×40 magnification), showing a papillomatous lesion with a predominance of epithelioid melanocytic cells, cellular maturation in the deep dermis, and absence of atypia or mitosis.
cleaned: Papillomatous lesion with predominantly epithelioid melanocytic cells, cellular maturation in the deep dermis, and absence of atypia or mitosis. Spitz nevus.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology from the cutaneous malignant melanoma of the scalp (primary tumor, 25 years prior) in a 72-year-old male patient, showing malignant cells with pagetoid spread into the epidermis (red arrow). The tumor cells are arranged in a nesting pattern in the dermis, displaying round nuclei and vacuolated and pink cytoplasm. A dermal host response with lymphocytic infiltrate is also visible. This case is notable for the later development of an isolated splenic metastasis 25 years after the initial diagnosis of cutaneous malignant melanoma. Hematoxylin and eosin-stained section, magnification × 20.
cleaned: Malignant cells with pagetoid spread into the epidermis. Tumor cells arranged in a nesting pattern in the dermis, displaying round nuclei and vacuolated pink cytoplasm. Dermal lymphocytic infiltrate is visible. Cutaneous malignant melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology from the spleen of a 72-year-old male patient, showing replacement of the splenic parenchyma by a malignant neoplasm, consistent with metastasis from cutaneous malignant melanoma. Residual lymphoid cells are observed at the periphery (black arrow). The patient presented with elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) levels and a solitary 4 × 3 × 3 cm solid lesion in the spleen, making this a rare case of isolated splenic metastasis 25 years after the initial diagnosis. Hematoxylin and eosin-stained section, magnification × 4.
cleaned: Splenic parenchyma replaced by a malignant neoplasm. Residual lymphoid cells are observed at the periphery. Diagnosis: metastasis from malignant melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology from the spleen of a 72-year-old male patient, showing replacement of the splenic parenchyma by a malignant neoplasm, consistent with metastasis from cutaneous malignant melanoma. Residual lymphoid cells are observed at the periphery (black arrow). The patient presented with elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) levels and a solitary 4 × 3 × 3 cm solid lesion in the spleen, making this a rare case of isolated splenic metastasis 25 years after the initial diagnosis. Hematoxylin and eosin-stained section, magnification × 10.
cleaned: Splenic parenchyma replaced by a malignant neoplasm. Residual lymphoid cells are observed at the periphery. Diagnosis: metastasis from malignant melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: High-power view histopathology from the spleen of a 72-year-old male patient, illustrating the morphology of the metastatic malignant neoplasm. It shows round, short spindly, and ovoid tumor cells arranged in vague solid and fascicular patterns, with pink and eosinophilic cytoplasm. This splenic lesion represents a rare isolated metastasis from a cutaneous malignant melanoma diagnosed 25 years prior. Hematoxylin and eosin-stained section, magnification × 40.
cleaned: Round, short spindly, and ovoid tumor cells arranged in vague solid and fascicular patterns, with pink and eosinophilic cytoplasm. Splenic lesion representing metastatic malignant melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Photomicrograph of H&E staining (200× magnification) showing atypical melanocyte proliferation with confluency and prominent pagetoid spread, consistent with subungual melanoma in situ. Histopathologic features for the diagnosis of subungual melanoma in situ include an uneven distribution of solitary melanocytes with irregularly scattered atypical melanocytes, increased cellular atypia, and prominent pagetoid spread.
cleaned:
Atypical melanocyte proliferation with confluency and prominent pagetoid spread. Irregularly scattered atypical melanocytes with increased cellular atypia.
Diagnosis: melanoma in situ.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology showing nests and diffuse collections of subepithelial nevus cells. The image displays entrapped epithelial inclusion cysts and the total absence of intraepithelial nevocytic junctional nests. The image is stained with hematoxylin-eosin, viewed at an original magnification of x100.
cleaned:
Nests and diffuse collections of subepithelial nevus cells. Entrapped epithelial inclusion cysts are present. Absence of intraepithelial nevocytic junctional nests. 

Nevus.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing melanoma, at 20× magnification, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E). Melanoma cells are observed extending in a perivascular fashion within the deep dermis. This perivascular infiltration is consistent with findings from the pathology report and is indicative of in-transit metastases, where the melanoma spreads out via proximal vasculature and lymphatics, reflecting the overall severity and thickness of this malignancy.
cleaned: Melanoma cells extending in a perivascular fashion within the deep dermis. Melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology (2× magnification, Hematoxylin and Eosin stain) showing a complete dermoepidermal separation with serum deposit between the dermis and epidermis. No melanoma is observed in the epidermis.
cleaned: Complete dermoepidermal separation with serum deposit between the dermis and epidermis.

label: nevus
original: Representative histopathological findings of a nevus (patient #15) showing nests of nevus cells in the dermis that are covered by, but do not touch, the normal epidermis, as observed with Hematoxylin–eosin staining.
cleaned: Nests of nevus cells in the dermis covered by normal epidermis. Diagnosis: nevus.

label: nevus
original: Histologic examination (hematoxylin and eosin, magnification, ×10) revealed proliferation of heavily pigmented melanocytes, mature nevus cells, and abundant melanin-laden macrophages in the underlying stroma, with multiple cysts, consistent with a pigmented cystic compound nevus of the conjunctiva. No evidence of pagetoid spread or dysplasia was detected.
cleaned: Proliferation of heavily pigmented melanocytes, mature nevus cells, and abundant melanin-laden macrophages in the underlying stroma, with multiple cysts. Pigmented cystic compound nevus.

label: nevus
original: Pathological image of an excisional biopsy demonstrating Intraocular Junctional Conjunctival Nevus (IJCN). Histopathology (Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, ×100 magnification) shows a prominent and dense infiltrate of nevus cells, characterized by mild nuclear atypia, within the conjunctival epithelium. Cyst formation is observed within the epithelium (yellow arrow). Additionally, there is dense lymphocyte infiltration forming a lymphoid follicle (red arrow).
cleaned: Dense infiltrate of nevus cells with mild nuclear atypia within the conjunctival epithelium. Cyst formation is observed within the epithelium. Dense lymphocyte infiltration forming a lymphoid follicle. Intraocular Junctional Conjunctival Nevus.

label: nevus
original: Pathological image of an excisional biopsy demonstrating Intraocular Junctional Conjunctival Nevus (IJCN). Histopathology (Hematoxylin and Eosin stain, ×200 magnification) at higher magnification reveals a congregation of small foci consisting of nevus cells with moderately irregular nuclei. These nevus cells show mild nuclear atypia and are part of the dense proliferation within the conjunctival epithelium, as observed in this case of IJCN.
cleaned: Congregation of small foci of nevus cells with moderately irregular nuclei. Nevus cells show mild nuclear atypia and are within a dense proliferation of the conjunctival epithelium. Intraocular Junctional Conjunctival Nevus.

label: nevus
original: A hematoxylin and eosin micrograph at 100x magnification from a biopsy confirming the diagnosis of Ota nevus. The image shows clusters of dendritic and fusiform melanocytes (indicated by black arrowheads) in the papillary dermis, diffusely infiltrating the collagen interstitium, around and near vessels (indicated by red asterisks). Melanophages (indicated by white arrowheads) are also observed in the dermis, extending into the reticular dermis, which collectively supported the pathological report of Ota nevus.
cleaned:
Clusters of dendritic and fusiform melanocytes infiltrating the papillary dermis, around and near vessels. Melanophages observed in the dermis, extending into the reticular dermis. 
Diagnosis: Ota nevus.

label: nevus
original: Histopathological representation of a nevus, stained with hematoxylin and eosin at 100x magnification. This image is associated with a 30-year-old woman who presented with a concern regarding an increase in the size of a pre-existing pigmented lesion in her right eye, which was clinically diagnosed as a nevus without features of malignant transformation.
cleaned: Nests of melanocytes with uniform morphology are present within the dermal layer. The lesion exhibits a symmetrical, rounded shape and contains pigmented cells. Tissue architecture is consistent with a nevus.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of a 61-year-old female's back, showing late-onset lentiginous and nested melanoma. The image reveals a chaotic architecture with variably sized nests and confluent single nevoid melanocytes that extend along broad junctional zones where the rete ridges are absent, producing a lentiginous pattern. Key features include loss of rete ridges and confluent melanocytes. This is an H&E stained digital scan at ×2 magnification.
cleaned: Chaotic architecture with variably sized nests and confluent single nevoid melanocytes extending along broad junctional zones; absent rete ridges producing a lentiginous pattern; loss of rete ridges and confluent melanocytes. Lentiginous and nested melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology from the chest of an eighty-year-old male showing a late-onset hypercellular nested melanoma with an accentuated rete ridge pattern. This panel highlights the hypercellular nests. The image is stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and is a digital scan taken at ×2 magnification.
cleaned: Hypercellular nests with an accentuated rete ridge pattern. Melanoma.

label: nevus
original: Hematoxylin and eosin stain (original magnification 10x) of a dysplastic nevus (DN) with mild atypia. Histologically, the image shows pigmented parakeratosis overlying a hyperplastic epidermis with lentigo-like features, which are frequently observed in DN with mild atypia. It is associated with a melanocytic proliferation displaying a discrete junctional proliferation with very small nests, irregularly distributed along the dermal–epidermal junction. Other features include a “shoulder sign”, a slight papillary fibroplasia, and a slight dermal lymphocytic infiltrate associated with melanophages.
cleaned: Pigmented parakeratosis overlies a hyperplastic epidermis with lentigo-like features. A melanocytic proliferation displays small nests irregularly distributed along the dermal–epidermal junction, with a “shoulder sign” and slight papillary fibroplasia. A slight dermal lymphocytic infiltrate is associated with melanophages. Diagnosis: dysplastic nevus.

label: nevus
original: Hematoxylin and eosin stain (original magnification 10x) of a dysplastic nevus (DN) with moderate atypia. The junctional proliferation shows small nests irregularly distributed along the dermal–epidermal junction, not completely filling the rete ridges. These medium-sized nests are observed histologically and likely correspond to the slightly larger pigmented network or small globi seen clinically.
cleaned: Small, irregularly distributed nests of medium-sized cells at the dermal–epidermal junction, not completely filling the rete ridges. Dysplastic nevus.

label: nevus
original: Hematoxylin and eosin stain (original magnification 15x) of a dysplastic nevus (DN) with moderate atypia, showing mild cytonuclear atypia. This higher magnification view also reveals papillary fibroplasia, a “shoulder sign”, and a slight dermal lymphocytic infiltrate associated with melanophages. The dermal component shows a superficial congenital architecture. These histological features are consistent with lesions that clinically present with a larger pigmented network or small globi.
cleaned: Dysplastic nevus with moderate atypia. Mild cytonuclear atypia is present. Papillary fibroplasia and a “shoulder sign” are visible. A slight dermal lymphocytic infiltrate associated with melanophages is noted. The dermal component shows a superficial congenital architecture.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of a melanoma in situ (hematoxylin and eosin stain, original magnification 10x), shown for comparison. The image reveals intraepidermal anarchic proliferation characterized by irregular nests, numerous solitary units, and pagetoid spread.
cleaned: Intraepidermal proliferation of atypical melanocytes with irregular nests, solitary units, and pagetoid spread. Diagnosis: melanoma in situ.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of a melanoma in situ (hematoxylin and eosin stain, original magnification 20x), shown for comparison. This higher magnification view details severe cytonuclear atypia, displaying very large cytoplasm and very large nuclei. A moderate dermal lymphocytic infiltrate associated with melanophages is also present. This panel highlights features seen in the intraepidermal anarchic proliferation described in panel B.
cleaned: Severe cytonuclear atypia with large cytoplasm and large nuclei. Moderate dermal lymphocytic infiltrate and melanophages are present. Intraepidermal anarchic proliferation. Melanoma in situ.

label: melanoma
original: Hematoxylin and eosin stain (original magnification 10x) of a superficial spreading melanoma (Breslow 0.3 mm), which is a microinvasive superficial spreading melanoma shown for comparison. The image demonstrates intraepidermal anarchic proliferation with irregular nests, numerous solitary units, and pagetoid spread.
cleaned:
Irregular nests and solitary units of atypical melanocytes with pagetoid spread within the epidermis. Intraepidermal anarchic proliferation.
Superficial spreading melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Hematoxylin and eosin stain (original magnification 20x) of a superficial spreading melanoma (Breslow 0.3 mm), which is a microinvasive superficial spreading melanoma shown for comparison. This panel highlights severe cytonuclear atypia, characterized by very large cytoplasm and very large nuclei. The same atypia is also present in a few nests in the superficial dermis (arrow). Additionally, there is a moderate dermal lymphocytic infiltrate associated with melanophages.
cleaned:
Nests of atypical melanocytes with large nuclei and abundant cytoplasm are present in the superficial dermis. Moderate dermal lymphocytic infiltrate and melanophages are visible.
Superficial spreading melanoma.

label: nevus
original: Hematoxylin and eosin stain (original magnification 10x) of a dysplastic nevus (DN) with marked atypia. The junctional component shows a continuous proliferation of solitary units along the dermal–epidermal junction, and some irregular nests. Histological features also include a slight papillary fibroplasia and a moderate dermal lymphocytic infiltrate associated with melanophages.
cleaned:
Continuous proliferation of solitary units and irregular nests along the dermal–epidermal junction. Slight papillary fibroplasia and moderate dermal lymphocytic infiltrate with melanophages.
Diagnosis: dysplastic nevus.

label: nevus
original: Hematoxylin and eosin stain (original magnification 20x) of a dysplastic nevus (DN) with marked atypia, showing marked cytonuclear atypia. The atypical features include large cytoplasm and large nuclei with hyperchromasia. This image provides a higher magnification view of the histological characteristics observed in a dysplastic nevus with marked atypia.
cleaned: Large cells with large, hyperchromatic nuclei and abundant cytoplasm. Marked cellular atypia. Dysplastic nevus.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of a dysplastic nevus (DN) with mild atypia, stained with Hematoxylin and eosin (original magnification 10x). This lesion is defined by mild cytological, nuclear, and nucleolar atypia. Architecturally, it shows a discrete junctional melanocytic proliferation with very small nests, irregularly distributed along the dermal–epidermal junction. There is pigmented parakeratosis overlying a hyperplastic epidermis with lentigo-like features and hyperpigmentation. Additional features include a slight “shoulder sign”, slight papillary fibroplasia, and a slight-to-moderate dermal lymphocytic infiltrate associated with melanophages.
cleaned: Discrete junctional melanocytic proliferation with very small nests irregularly distributed along the dermal–epidermal junction. Pigmented parakeratosis overlies a hyperplastic epidermis with lentigo-like features and hyperpigmentation. Slight “shoulder sign” and slight papillary fibroplasia are present. Slight-to-moderate dermal lymphocytic infiltrate associated with melanophages. Dysplastic nevus.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of a dysplastic nevus (DN) with moderate atypia, stained with Hematoxylin and eosin (original magnification 10x). This lesion is defined by mild cytological, nuclear, and nucleolar atypia. Architecturally, it shows a more pronounced junctional proliferation with well-developed, irregularly distributed nests, which do not completely fill the rete ridges. Other features include a “shoulder sign”, papillary fibroplasia, and a slight dermal lymphocytic infiltrate associated with melanophages.
cleaned:
Irregularly distributed nests of cells proliferate at the dermal-epidermal junction, with some nests not completely filling the rete ridges. Cells exhibit mild atypia. Papillary fibroplasia and a slight dermal lymphocytic infiltrate with melanophages are present. 

Diagnosis: Dysplastic nevus.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of a dysplastic nevus (DN) with distinct atypia, specifically of the “nested type”, stained with Hematoxylin and eosin (original magnification 10x). This lesion is defined by more pronounced cytological and nucleolar atypia, presenting as moderate cytonuclear atypia. Architecturally, the junctional component shows large, irregularly distributed, voluminous nests completely filling the rete ridges. Other features include a “shoulder sign”, papillary fibroplasia, and a slight-to-moderate dermal lymphocytic infiltrate associated with melanophages. There is no suprabasal melanocyte proliferation and no solitary units between the rete ridges.
cleaned: Irregularly distributed, voluminous nests of cells completely filling the rete ridges. Moderate cytonuclear atypia with prominent nucleoli. Papillary fibroplasia and a dermal lymphocytic infiltrate with melanophages are present. No suprabasal melanocyte proliferation or solitary units between the rete ridges. Diagnosis: dysplastic nevus.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of a dysplastic nevus (DN) with distinct atypia, specifically of the “solitary unit type”, stained with Hematoxylin and eosin (original magnification 10x). This lesion is defined by more pronounced cytological and nucleolar atypia, presenting as moderate cytonuclear atypia. Architecturally, it shows lentiginous hyperplasia and hyperpigmentation of the epidermis, and a junctional melanocytic proliferation with solitary units completely filling the rete ridges. There are no signs of continuous proliferation or pagetoid spread, no suprabasal melanocyte proliferation, and no solitary units between the rete ridges.
cleaned: Lentiginous hyperplasia and hyperpigmentation of the epidermis with a junctional melanocytic proliferation completely filling the rete ridges as solitary units. Moderate cytonuclear atypia is present. Diagnosis: dysplastic nevus.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology stained with Hematoxylin and eosin (original magnification 10x), depicting a Dysplastic Nevus (DN) with marked atypia. This condition is defined by marked cytological, nuclear, and nucleolar atypia, and architecturally by a continuous proliferation along the dermal–epidermal junction, or with solitary units between the rete ridges, eventually with the presence of few suprabasal melanocytes, but only focally. The junctional component in this image shows marked cytonuclear atypia, specifically with marked nuclear hyperchromasia, and a continuous proliferation along the dermal–epidermal junction, with solitary units between the rete ridges.
cleaned: Atypical melanocytes with hyperchromatic nuclei proliferate along the dermal–epidermal junction and as solitary units between rete ridges. Dysplastic Nevus.

label: nevus
original: Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain (original magnification 10x) of a dysplastic nevus (DN) with distinct atypia, nested type. Junctional proliferation shows large and voluminous nests, probably representing the larger nests observed histologically, irregularly distributed along the dermal–epidermal junction, completely filling the rete ridges, with moderate cytonuclear atypia. A slight papillary fibroplasia, a “shoulder sign”, and a slight dermal lymphocytic infiltrate associated with melanophages are observed.
cleaned:
Nests of cells irregularly distributed along the dermal–epidermal junction, completely filling the rete ridges. Cells exhibit moderate atypia. Slight papillary fibroplasia and a dermal lymphocytic infiltrate with melanophages are observed.
Diagnosis: dysplastic nevus.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of a dysplastic nevus (DN) with severe atypia, stained with Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). The image shows the junctional component with a very disorganized proliferation, including solitary units and irregular nests. These histological characteristics contribute to the classification of DN with severe atypia, a lesion type that may present clinically without major or specific differences compared to superficial borderline atypical melanocytic lesions. (Original magnification 10x).
cleaned: Disorganized proliferation of solitary units and irregular nests at the junction. Dysplastic nevus with severe atypia.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of a dysplastic nevus (DN) with severe atypia, likely stained with Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), showing marked and/or severe cytonuclear atypia. The cells display abundant cytoplasm, and large and irregular nuclei with hyperchromasia. Additional features include a “shoulder sign”, papillary fibroplasia, and a moderate dermal lymphocytic infiltrate associated with melanophages. These findings are consistent with DN with severe atypia, which can be challenging to differentiate clinically from superficial borderline atypical melanocytic lesions. (Original magnification 20x).
cleaned: Cells display abundant cytoplasm and large, irregular, hyperchromatic nuclei. A “shoulder sign” and papillary fibroplasia are present. Moderate dermal lymphocytic infiltrate with melanophages is visible. Dysplastic nevus with severe atypia.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of an initial biopsy showing a compound dysplastic nevus. The image displays a nested junctional component with bridging and concentric lamellar fibroplasia around rete ridges. The dermal component is composed of small bland epithelioid melanocytes and shows maturation with depth. (Hematoxylin and eosin stain, 100× magnification).
cleaned: Compound dysplastic nevus. Nested junctional component with bridging and concentric lamellar fibroplasia around rete ridges. Dermal component composed of small bland epithelioid melanocytes showing maturation with depth.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology image at a closer inspection, revealing that the majority of the cells are arranged in sheets and show abundant grey cytoplasm with numerous associated melanophages. Smaller cells present in tight nests and resembling ordinary nevus are also present. The image is stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E).
cleaned: Sheets of cells with abundant grey cytoplasm and numerous melanophages. Smaller cells in tight nests resembling nevus cells are also present.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing melanoma underlying the respiratory mucosa of the sinonasal area, stained with hematoxylin & eosin and viewed at 10x magnification.
cleaned: Melanoma underlying respiratory mucosa. Tumor cells exhibit atypical morphology.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing melanoma with prominent atypia, mitosis, and melanin pigments. Stained with Hematoxylin & eosin (x20 magnification).
cleaned: Melanoma with prominent atypia, mitosis, and melanin pigments.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology at 400x magnification showing an H&E stained section. The image features an intact epidermis with a focal underlying inflammatory infiltrate. In the deep dermis and subcutaneous tissue, a nodular lesion is present, composed of a cystic space adjacent to a proliferation of diffuse nests of atypical cells and marked necrosis extending into subcutaneous tissue without epidermal involvement. This histopathological examination contributed to confirming the diagnosis of invasive malignant melanoma.
cleaned: Intact epidermis with focal underlying inflammatory infiltrate. Nodular lesion in deep dermis and subcutaneous tissue composed of a cystic space adjacent to diffuse nests of atypical cells and marked necrosis extending into subcutaneous tissue. Invasive malignant melanoma.

label: melanoma

cleaned: Symmetrical, compound melanocytic lesion extending into the superficial dermis with large nests at the junction. Few melanocytes are visible in the epidermis. Hyperplastic, hyperkeratotic epidermal changes are present. Superficial spreading melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of a superficial spreading melanoma (SSM) from case 2, stained with H&E at ×40 magnification. The image details epithelioid to spindle-shaped melanocytes with pale, eosinophilic cytoplasm. There is variation in the size and shape of the cells, which exhibit large nuclei with prominent nucleoli and nuclear vacuoles, indicating slight nuclear atypia. A few multinucleated cells are also visible, consistent with the detailed microscopical findings for this case.
cleaned: Epithelioid to spindle-shaped melanocytes with pale, eosinophilic cytoplasm. Variation in cell size and shape with large nuclei containing prominent nucleoli and nuclear vacuoles. Few multinucleated cells are visible. Superficial spreading melanoma.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of a Variant Spitz nevus from the left upper leg of a 48-year-old female, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at ×5 magnification. The image displays a symmetrical, compound melanocytic lesion with nests and solitary melanocytes at the dermo-epidermal junction. The dermal component shows a partly naevoid aspect. Melanocytes are observed spreading out between desmoplastic stroma at the base of the lesion, which also features hyperplastic, hyper- and parakeratotic epidermis, and moderate nuclear atypia.
cleaned: Symmetrical, compound melanocytic lesion with nests and solitary melanocytes at the dermo-epidermal junction. The dermal component shows a partly naevoid aspect. Melanocytes are observed spreading out between desmoplastic stroma at the base of the lesion, which also features hyperplastic, hyper- and parakeratotic epidermis, and moderate nuclear atypia. Variant Spitz nevus.

label: nevus
original: High-magnification histopathology of a Variant Spitz nevus from the left upper leg of a 48-year-old female, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at ×40 magnification. This image shows epithelioid to slightly spindle-shaped melanocytes with pale, eosinophilic, and partly pigmented cytoplasm. The cells exhibit enlarged nuclei and prominent nucleoli, characteristic of the moderate nuclear atypia observed in this case. A single mitotic figure was noted in the upper dermis.
cleaned: Epithelioid and slightly spindle-shaped melanocytes with pale, eosinophilic, and partly pigmented cytoplasm. Enlarged nuclei with prominent nucleoli. Single mitotic figure in the upper dermis. Variant Spitz nevus.

label: melanoma
original: Representative image of a biopsy specimen taken from the tympanic membrane of a 68-year-old male patient presenting with left-sided, progressive hearing loss and an atypical granulomatous presentation. cleaned: Nests and sheets of highly atypical melanocytic proliferation within a fibrous background. Mucosal melanoma.

label: melanoma

cleaned: Atypical melanocytic proliferation with epithelioid cells exhibiting eosinophilic and vacuolated cytoplasm. Nuclei are anisomorphic with prominent macronucleoli and show mitotic activity. Mucosal melanoma.

label: nevus

label: nevus

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of the resected lesion showing microscopic examination confirmed melanoma, stained with Hematoxylin and eosin (x100).

label: melanoma

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology (hematoxylin and eosin, H&E stain) showing diffuse plasma cells (CD138 positive) in a representative case of successful treatment for melanoma with talimogene laherparepvec (T-VEC). This image demonstrates a complete response, with no residual melanoma tumor tissue observed after 12 months of T-VEC treatment. Plasma cells can be recognized by their oval shape, round and eccentric nucleus with coarse chromatin, a prominent perinuclear hof, and abundant basophilic cytoplasm. These plasma cell infiltrates were not present prior to intralesional T-VEC injections and were observed to form after T-VEC start, being present in varying degrees in all biopsies from T-VEC injected sites. The relative degree of plasma cells (CD138) in such cases was comparable to cytotoxic T cells (CD8).
cleaned: Diffuse plasma cells with oval shape, round and eccentric nucleus with coarse chromatin, a prominent perinuclear hof, and abundant basophilic cytoplasm. Complete absence of melanoma tumor tissue. Plasma cell infiltrates.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology (hematoxylin and eosin, H&E stain) showing angiocentric features, part of a classical HSV-associated histopathological pattern. This image is from a representative case of successful treatment for melanoma with talimogene laherparepvec (T-VEC), demonstrating a complete response with no residual melanoma tumor tissue after 12 months of treatment. Angiocentric features were significantly more frequently identified in patients with complete response (>6 months) compared with patients without a durable response (75% vs. 29%, P = 0.015), indicating their association with a positive treatment outcome.
cleaned: Angiocentric features. Tissue architecture shows no residual tumor.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology (hematoxylin and eosin, H&E stain) showing perineural features, part of a classical HSV-associated histopathological pattern. This image is from a representative case of successful treatment for melanoma with talimogene laherparepvec (T-VEC), demonstrating a complete response with no residual melanoma tumor tissue after 12 months of treatment. Perineural features were observed in conjunction with diffuse plasma cells, angiocentric features, and granulomas in this successful treatment outcome. In biopsies from T-VEC injected sites, localization of immune infiltrates, which include these features, shifted from solely superficial to deep, occasionally in combination with superficial patterns.
cleaned: Perineural features with diffuse plasma cells, angiocentric features, and granulomas. No residual tumor tissue is visible. Immune infiltrates are present both superficially and deeply.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology (hematoxylin and eosin, H&E stain) showing granulomas, part of a classical HSV-associated histopathological pattern. This image is from a representative case of successful treatment for melanoma with talimogene laherparepvec (T-VEC), demonstrating a complete response with no residual melanoma tumor tissue after 12 months of treatment. These 'classical' granulomas were significantly more frequently identified in patients with complete response (>6 months) compared with patients without a durable response (58% vs. 15%, P = 0.041), highlighting their association with a positive treatment outcome.
cleaned: Granulomas with surrounding tissue architecture. Complete absence of atypical cells or tumor nests.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of a biopsy from a 46-year-old woman who presented with a painless, verrucous lesion of the scalp. The image displays features of an invasive melanoma with prominent verrucous morphology. Microscopic examination reveals papilliferous acanthosis and hyperplasia of the epidermis, characterized by elongated rete ridges and orthohyperkeratosis. Atypical fragmented melanocytes, demonstrating anisokaryosis, are primarily arranged in nests along the dermis. The melanoma extends to the base and peripheral edges of the biopsy. The lesion was subsequently identified as a superficially spreading melanoma with a Breslow depth of 2 mm and Clark Level III, accompanied by brisk tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes. (Hematoxylin and eosin, ×20 magnification).
cleaned: Papilliferous acanthosis and hyperplasia of the epidermis with elongated rete ridges and orthohyperkeratosis. Atypical fragmented melanocytes with anisokaryosis arranged in nests along the dermis. The lesion extends to the base and peripheral edges of the biopsy. Invasive melanoma, superficially spreading melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of an office biopsy specimen, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E). The biopsy report indicated a malignant melanoma with no definite presence of ulceration. Lymphovascular or perineural invasion was not demonstrated, and focal changes suggestive of a regression phenomenon were present. The report also noted one mitosis per square millimeter and a minimal inflammatory response, with no evidence of a precursor melanocytic lesion. Complete excision of the lesion with appropriate margins of normal surrounding tissue was recommended.
cleaned: Spindle and epithelioid cells with hyperchromatic nuclei are arranged in nests and fascicles infiltrating the dermis. Scattered atypical melanocytes with prominent nucleoli are present. Focal areas of tumor regression with lymphocytic infiltrate are seen.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing an area of transition into heterologous chondroid differentiation within a melanoma. The diagnosis of melanoma was confirmed by immunoreactivity with SOX10 and HMB45. (Hematoxylin & eosin, ×400).
cleaned: Melanoma with areas of heterologous chondroid differentiation, displaying transition into osteocartilaginous differentiation, invading soft tissues and bone.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing an area of transition into heterologous osteoid differentiation within a melanoma, consistent with the presence of heterologous osteocartilaginous differentiation. cleaned: Melanoma with areas of heterologous osteoid and cartilaginous differentiation invading soft tissues and bone. Cells show malignant morphology with transition into osteoid differentiation.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology showing melanoma cells characterized by atypia, pleomorphism, evident nucleoli, and frequent mitotic figures. (Hematoxylin & eosin, ×400).
cleaned: Melanoma cells with atypia, pleomorphism, evident nucleoli, and frequent mitotic figures, invading soft tissues and bone.

label: nevus
original: Histopathological findings of a Reed nevus on the finger, showing several small tumor cell nests scattered within the epidermis. label: melanoma
original: Histopathology of nodular melanoma, with a 1.47 mm Breslow index and a vertical growth phase. The image displays a densely cellular melanocytic tumor, consisting of round/oval cells and rare, elongated, bulky cells. Tumor cells present pleiomorphic, atypical, hyperchromatic nuclei, with rare mitoses and brown pigment within the cytoplasm. These cells are organized in nests at the dermo-epidermal junction or in islands within the dermis. Predominantly, lymphocytic infiltrates are present, and the lesion exhibits positive regression. Ulceration, lymphovascular, and perineural invasion were noted to be absent in the pathology report. The melanoma diagnosis was further confirmed by immunohistochemistry. Stained with Hematoxylin and eosin, original magnification: ×100.
cleaned: Nodular melanoma. Densely cellular melanocytic tumor composed of round to oval cells and rare, elongated cells. Cells exhibit pleiomorphic, atypical, hyperchromatic nuclei with rare mitoses and brown cytoplasmic pigment. Tumor cells are arranged in nests at the dermo-epidermal junction and islands within the dermis. Lymphocytic infiltrates are present, with evidence of regression.

label: nevus
original: Histopathological findings showing cells with nevic appearance and epithelioid morphology. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain, x 40 magnification.
cleaned: Cells with nevic appearance and epithelioid morphology. Nevus.

label: nevus
original: Histopathological findings showing on the base a focus with nests of a conventional nevus. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stain, x 20 magnification.
cleaned: Nests of cells with a conventional nevus architecture.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology illustrating the various morphologic appearances of neoplastic cells in anorectal melanoma. Panel A shows epithelioid melanoma cells characterized by roundish nuclei and wide eosinophilic cytoplasm. Epithelioid cells, either alone or in combination with other cell types, were a common finding in the systematically reviewed cases of anorectal melanoma in this study. The tumors frequently presented as polypoid (73%) and most (67%) demonstrated pigmentation. The mean tumor thickness across cases was 10.4 mm, and ulceration was observed in 80% of cases. (Hematoxylin & Eosin, x400 magnification).
cleaned: Roundish nuclei with wide eosinophilic cytoplasm neoplastic cells. Polypoid lesion shape with pigmentation frequently observed.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology illustrating the various morphologic appearances of neoplastic cells in anorectal melanoma. Panel B displays lymphoma-like small neoplastic cells, which are admixed in a fibrous stroma and show crush artifact. Lymphoma-like cell types, either alone or combined with other morphologies, were observed in a notable percentage of the anorectal melanoma cases reviewed in this study. The tumors frequently presented as polypoid (73%) and most (67%) demonstrated pigmentation. The mean tumor thickness across cases was 10.4 mm, and ulceration was observed in 80% of cases. (Hematoxylin & Eosin, x400 magnification).
cleaned: Small, neoplastic cells admixed in fibrous stroma with crush artifact. Polypoid lesion with pigmentation.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology illustrating the various morphologic appearances of neoplastic cells in anorectal melanoma. Pleomorphic cell types, often in conjunction with epithelioid cells, were present in a subset of the anorectal melanoma cases reviewed in this study. The tumors frequently presented as polypoid (73%) and most (67%) demonstrated pigmentation. The mean tumor thickness across cases was 10.4 mm, and ulceration was observed in 80% of cases. (Hematoxylin & Eosin, x400 magnification).
cleaned: Pleomorphic melanoma cells with huge, bizarre nuclei and prominent cytoplasm. Pleomorphic and epithelioid cell types are present. Tumors frequently present as polypoid and demonstrate pigmentation.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology illustrating the various morphologic appearances of neoplastic cells in anorectal melanoma. Spindle cell types, either alone or in combination with other cell types, were a frequent finding in the systematically reviewed cases of anorectal melanoma in this study. The tumors frequently presented as polypoid (73%) and most (67%) demonstrated pigmentation. The mean tumor thickness across cases was 10.4 mm, and ulceration was observed in 80% of cases. label: melanoma

original: Histopathological photomicrograph stained with hematoxylin-eosin, showing the neoplastic population composed of sheets of melanoma cells intermingled with islands of squamous cells. The melanoma cells are epithelioid to spindle-shaped, with markedly atypical nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and eosinophilic cytoplasm. The squamous cells are atypical and show central horn pearl formations, true keratinization, dyskeratinocytes, and focal clear cell changes. Significant melanin pigment deposition is observed throughout the lesion, and brisk mitotic activity is present in both components. Original magnification 10×, scale bar 250 µm.

Significant melanin pigment deposition is observed throughout the lesion, and brisk mitotic activity is present.

Diagnosis: melanoma, squamous cells.

original: Histopathological photomicrograph stained with hematoxylin-eosin, showing the neoplastic population composed of sheets of melanoma cells intermingled with islands of squamous cells. label: melanoma
original: Histopathology (H&E, ×20 magnification) from a 28-year-old man presenting with a superficial spreading invasive melanoma. The image shows atypical melanocytes arranged in irregular nests and single units within the epidermis, along with sheets of atypical melanocytes infiltrating the dermis. These features are accompanied by a prominent lymphohistiocytic infiltrate, consistent with the diagnosis of a superficial spreading invasive melanoma.
cleaned: Atypical melanocytes arranged in irregular nests and single units within the epidermis, with sheets of atypical melanocytes infiltrating the dermis. A prominent lymphohistiocytic infiltrate is present. Superficial spreading invasive melanoma.

label: melanoma

label: melanoma
original: Histopathology (H&E, ×20 magnification) from a 28-year-old man presenting with a superficial spreading invasive melanoma. The image displays atypical melanocytes in irregular nests and single units within the epidermis and in sheets within the dermis, accompanied by a lymphohistiocytic infiltrate. Interestingly, a hair follicle with sebaceous glands (red arrow) is present in the upper part of the reticular dermis, representing hyperplastic sebaceous glands that were almost attached to the epidermis.

Atypical melanocytes in irregular nests and single units within the epidermis and in sheets within the dermis, accompanied by a lymphohistiocytic infiltrate. A hair follicle with sebaceous glands is present in the upper reticular dermis.
Invasive melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: A histological section (Hematoxylin & eosin, ×40 magnification) shows the primary melanoma in vertical growth. The vertical growth phase is a characteristic feature of melanoma, and while the growth phase itself has been losing value as a mandatory reporting parameter in recent years, the overall histological type of melanoma is still crucial for assessment. This primary tumor is integral for determining key staging parameters such as the Breslow index (maximum tumor thickness) and the presence or absence of ulceration.
cleaned: Vertical growth phase with atypical melanocytes exhibiting pleomorphism and hyperchromatic nuclei. Lesion displays irregular architecture and nests of melanocytes extending into the dermis. Diagnosis: melanoma.

label: melanoma
original: A histological section (Hematoxylin & eosin, ×40 magnification) shows a melanoma microsatellite in the dermis. This microsatellite is characterized by discontinuous tumor proliferation, located adjacent to the main tumor and permeated by normal tissue, without findings of inflammatory infiltrate or fibrosis that could suggest tumor regression. The presence or absence of satellitosis, which includes microsatellites, is a mandatory parameter that must be included in melanoma excision reports according to current international recommendations.
cleaned: Discontinuous tumor proliferation in the dermis, adjacent to a larger tumor mass, permeated by normal tissue. No inflammatory infiltrate or fibrosis is present. Melanoma microsatellite.

label: nevus
original: Panoramic view of an intradermal melanocytic nevus, removed for aesthetic purposes from a 14-year-old female patient. This case exemplifies the critical importance of anatomical-clinical correlation in diagnosis, as the interpretation of microscopic findings can be significantly influenced by the patient's age. The image is stained with Hematoxylin & eosin at ×40 magnification.
cleaned:
Nests of melanocytes within the dermal layer. The melanocytes exhibit uniform, round nuclei and moderate cytoplasm. The lesion is well-circumscribed and composed of melanocytic proliferation. 
Diagnosis: Melanocytic nevus.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of an intradermal melanocytic nevus, showing melanocytes arranged in nests with maturation to depth. This lesion was removed for aesthetic purposes from a 14-year-old female patient. Maturation to depth is a key feature indicating a benign melanocytic lesion, which is particularly relevant in pediatric patients where microscopic findings are carefully correlated with age for accurate diagnosis. The image is stained with Hematoxylin & eosin at ×100 magnification.
cleaned: Melanocytes arranged in nests with maturation to depth. Intradermal melanocytic nevus.

label: nevus
original: Histopathology of an intradermal melanocytic nevus, demonstrating no signs of atypia within the melanocytes. This finding supports the benign diagnosis of the lesion, which was removed for aesthetic purposes from a 14-year-old female patient. The absence of atypia, combined with the patient's age and clinical presentation, reinforces the importance of anatomical-clinical correlation in dermatopathology. The image is stained with Hematoxylin & eosin at ×200 magnification.
cleaned: Intradermal melanocytic nevus. Symmetrical proliferation of melanocytes within the dermis. Melanocytes exhibit uniform, round nuclei and no significant cytologic atypia. No mitoses are identified.

label: nevus
original: High magnification view showing the presence of atypical deep mitosis in the center of an intradermal melanocytic nevus. Despite this atypical finding, the overall diagnosis remains benign for this lesion, which was removed for aesthetic purposes from a 14-year-old female patient. This highlights the crucial role of anatomical-clinical correlation, especially in pediatric cases where certain microscopic features that might be concerning in an adult may not alter the benign diagnosis when correlated with the patient's age and clinical context. The image is stained with Hematoxylin & eosin at ×1000 magnification.
cleaned: Intradermal melanocytic nevus with atypical deep mitosis. Spindle and epithelioid melanocytes are present.

label: nevus
original: Panoramic image of a traumatized nevus located on the thigh of a 44-year-old female patient. The image shows the remaining nevus on the left, with central fibrosis and a dermal inflammatory infiltrate including melanophages on the right. This case exemplifies the importance of clinical-pathological correlation, as the patient's age (44 years old) and location of the lesion (thigh) are critical data points for interpreting microscopic findings, especially in the context of reported trauma to the lesion. The image is stained with Hematoxylin & eosin at ×40 magnification.
cleaned: Remaining nevus on the left with central fibrosis. Dermal inflammatory infiltrate including melanophages on the right.

label: nevus
original: Detailed view of the remaining intradermal nevus and the fibrosis, which runs parallel to the direction of the epidermis. This image provides a higher magnification of features seen in the traumatized nevus from a 44-year-old female patient's thigh. The presence of fibrosis is consistent with a traumatized melanocytic lesion, highlighting the need for comprehensive clinical correlation, including patient age, lesion location, and any history of trauma, which can influence microscopic interpretation. The image is stained with Hematoxylin & eosin at ×200 magnification.
cleaned: Intradermal nevus with parallel fibrosis running alongside the epidermis. Fibrotic tissue is visible.

label: nevus
original: Detailed view of the inflammatory infiltrate, which includes melanophages. This image provides a higher magnification of features seen in the traumatized nevus from a 44-year-old female patient's thigh. An inflammatory infiltrate with melanophages is a common finding in traumatized melanocytic lesions, reinforcing the need for clinical-pathological correlation, where the clinical history of trauma helps in the accurate interpretation of these microscopic features. The image is stained with Hematoxylin & eosin at ×200 magnification.
cleaned: Inflammatory infiltrate with melanophages. Melanocytic lesion.

label: nevus
original: Presence of atypical proliferated junctional melanocytes, a common finding in traumatized melanocytic lesions. This feature is observed in the context of a traumatized nevus from a 44-year-old female patient's thigh. The occurrence of trauma is a crucial piece of clinical information, as it can lead to microscopic changes like atypical junctional melanocytes that might simulate malignancy but are benign in the context of trauma. Such findings underscore the necessity of integrating clinical history (e.g., occurrence of trauma) with microscopic examination for accurate diagnosis. The image is stained with Hematoxylin & eosin at ×400 magnification.
cleaned: Proliferation of atypical junctional melanocytes.

label: melanoma
original: Histopathological section from the excised parotid gland lesion, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) at 20× magnification. Microscopic analysis further revealed nuclear pleomorphism and prominent nucleoli, characteristic features of the malignant neoplasm.
cleaned: Spindle-shaped malignant cells with nuclear pleomorphism and prominent nucleoli. Spindle cell melanoma.

original: Histopathology of an HRAS-mutated spitzoid tumor from a 47-year-old female patient. The lesion, located on the right lower arm and measuring 0.8 cm in diameter, was initially diagnosed as Spitzoid Tumor of Uncertain Malignant Potential (STUMP). This image, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (HE) at 25× magnification, shows the lesion to be symmetrical, wedge-shaped, and primarily dermal-located. It is characterized by epithelioid spitzoid-looking cells embedded in an eosinophilic hyalinized stroma. A count revealed 2 mitoses present per 1 mm², with an absence of deep mitoses. HRAS-mutated spitzoid melanocytic tumors, like this one, have sometimes been initially misdiagnosed as melanoma based on histology alone, especially in adults displaying frequent or deep mitotic activity, though they typically show a good prognosis with no recurrences or metastases over long-term follow-up.

Diagnosis: Spitzoid Tumor of Uncertain Malignant Potential (STUMP).

label: melanoma
original: This hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stained image displays an area within a proliferative nodule of a cutaneous congenital melanocytic naevus (CMN). It features typical small deep melanocytes intermixed with expansile regions composed of spindled cells and areas containing larger cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm. Crucially, there is no significant atypia and an absence of mitoses, which is characteristic of benign proliferative nodules in CMN. Such nodules are generally defined histopathologically by a mixture of morphological cell types and a pushing border that may distort surrounding structures. In contrast to melanoma, they typically lack necrosis, significant cytological atypia, or high proliferative activity, and documented progression of a clearly benign nodule to malignancy is difficult to find.
cleaned:
Small, deep melanocytes intermixed with expansile regions of spindled cells and larger cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm. Proliferative nodule with a pushing border distorting surrounding structures. No significant atypia or mitoses are seen. 

Cutaneous congenital melanocytic naevus.

label: melanoma
original: This hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stained image shows a section of a proliferative nodule within a cutaneous congenital melanocytic naevus (CMN). The image reveals typical small deep melanocytes alongside expansile areas formed by spindled cells and regions with larger cells possessing eosinophilic cytoplasm. Notably, no significant cytological atypia and no mitotic figures are observed. These features are consistent with a benign proliferative nodule, which is characterized by a diverse mix of cell types and a pushing border, and typically lacks the necrosis, marked atypia, or high proliferative activity associated with melanoma. Although these nodules can be quite proliferative, cases of benign nodules transforming into malignancy are rarely documented.
cleaned: Nodule with small, deep melanocytes and expansile areas of spindled cells. Regions contain larger cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm. No significant cytological atypia or mitotic figures are observed. Benign proliferative nodule. Congenital melanocytic naevus.

label: melanoma
original: This hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stained image illustrates the histological characteristics of a proliferative nodule found within a cutaneous congenital melanocytic naevus (CMN). It demonstrates areas composed of typical small deep melanocytes, admixed with expansile zones of spindled cells, and regions of larger cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm. Importantly, there is an absence of significant cytological atypia and no mitoses are seen, indicating its benign nature. Benign proliferative nodules typically feature a diverse cellular composition and a pushing border, and, unlike melanoma, generally do not exhibit necrosis, marked cytological atypia, or increased proliferative activity. Despite suggestions that these nodules might be precursors to melanoma, convincing documentation of such progression is uncommon in the literature.
cleaned:
Nodule composed of small, deep melanocytes and expansile zones of spindled cells, with regions of larger cells containing eosinophilic cytoplasm. No significant cytological atypia or mitoses are seen. The nodule has a pushing border. 

Congenital melanocytic naevus, benign proliferative nodule.

original: Histopathology image of a superficial spreading melanoma (T1a, Breslow 0.5 mm) on the abdomen of an 82-year-old man, stained with Hematoxylin and Eosin (HE) at 40× original magnification. Superficial spreading melanoma is a slow-growing melanoma, and regression (white areas) and inflammation may be present at any stage. Atypical melanocytes in single units and irregular nests mostly arranged at the dermo-epidermal junction (DEJ), along with melanophages in the dermis, correlate with structureless bichromatic brown-blue areas (labeled +).
